output.text:
Script completed
Wall time 0.2 seconds
Output:

Warning: truncated output (original token count: 15644)
Total output lines: 689

Playwright-generated documentation is primarily user/admin product documentation. It also provides useful verification evidence for future agents.

## Out of Scope for Now

Avoid adding these without an explicit product decision:

- anonymous or guest registration without an account
- private invite-only events
- complex strict waitlist reservation queues
- automatic event archival
- push notifications
- sophisticated budgeting and receipt-category planning
- hard-coded ESN-only assumptions
- rigid requirements/test ID matrices

## Design Watchpoints

### Permission dependencies

Current default: permissions are capabilities with admin-facing names and descriptions. Some capabilities imply access to related data.

Raise this when: changing authorization, event review, publishing, registration management, tenant settings, or role configuration.

Do not: make authorization more permissive to unblock a UI flow.

### Event archival

Current default: build the data model so archived event records can retain non-personal event history while removing user personal data where possible.

Raise this when: changing event, registration, payment, receipt, or check-in persistence.

Do not: add automatic archival behavior without an explicit product decision.

### Legal pages

Current default: tenants provide tenant-specific legal/privacy/imprint/terms information, possibly alongside platform-provided sections.

Raise this when: changing tenant onboarding, legal page rendering, domains, privacy copy, or production readiness.

Do not: invent generic legal fallback text and treat it as production-ready.

### Registration complexity

Current default: simple UI first, flexible registration-option model underneath.

Raise this when: adding new registration modes, discounts, eligibility logic, capacity handling, guest quantities, or waitlist behavior.
**Evidence**

- `src/shared/rpc-contracts/app-rpcs/users.rpcs.ts:44-49` accepts only name and communication-email fields for account creation.
- `src/app/core/create-account/create-account.component.html:20-72` presents only those fields.
- `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/users.handlers.ts:317-461` immediately creates the tenant membership and default roles after authentication.
- `src/db/schema/users.ts:20-110` has neither a home-tenant relation nor tenant-onboarding acceptance/answer records.

**Fix direction**

Model versioned privacy acceptance and tenant-scoped required-question answers.
Collect and validate them before the membership transaction, preserve their audit
trail, and make that first completed membership the home tenant. Add the
home-tenant warning and an explicit profile action to change it; a later
automatic cross-tenant join must never overwrite it. Every policy change must
require re-acceptance and inform the tenant administrator who made the change.
Support only short-text and selection-list tenant questions. Resolve current
requirements for every authenticated tenant user and require immediate
completion when information or policy acceptance is missing.

### TEN-001 — Fixed formatting locale and tenant currency/timezone are not applied at runtime

**Severity:** P1
**Why it matters:** The product uses fixed `de-DE` formatting while currency and business timezone remain tenant settings. The current application provides the tenant currency to Angular, but does not consistently apply the fixed locale or tenant timezone.

**Evidence**

- `src/db/schema/tenants.ts:23-71` stores currency, locale, and timezone, but the current tenant-locales enum does not include fixed `de-DE`.
- `src/app/app.config.ts:89-92` supplies `DEFAULT_CURRENCY_CODE`, but does not provide tenant `LOCALE_ID`, a date/time default zone, or Luxon zone configuration.
- `src/app/admin/general-settings/general-settings.component.ts:230-247` reloads after a locale/timezone edit even though the runtime does not apply those values consistently.
- `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/admin.handlers.ts:162-205` locks tenant-admin edits after event/payment data exists, while `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/global-admin.handlers.ts:100-190` has no equivalent guarded/audited override path.

**Fix direction**

Fix the formatting locale to `de-DE` and remove it as a tenant-editable choice.
Support the Section App currency set (`EUR`, `CZK`, `AUD`) and IANA tenant
timezones with `Europe/Berlin` as the default. Wire the fixed locale, tenant
currency, and tenant timezone safely through SSR, Angular/Material formatting,
Luxon conversion, and server-side business-time calculations. Keep recorded
transaction currency and event instants immutable; make any post-data
platform-admin override explicit and auditable.

### ADMIN-001 — Platform administrator policy exceeds the current tenant-membership boundary

**Severity:** P1
**Why it matters:** The accepted policy lets platform administrators perform any tenant operation at any time. The current runtime grants broad permissions, but the normal tenant UI and user-context flows still require a tenant assignment.

**Evidence**

- `src/server/context/request-context-resolver.ts:42-58` grants a global administrator `ALL_PERMISSIONS` and `globalAdmin:manageTenants`.
- `src/app/app.routes.ts:14-46` wraps normal event, admin, finance, profile, and scanner routes in `userAccountGuard`; only `/global-admin` avoids that guard.
- `src/app/core/guards/user-account.guard.ts:8-18` redirects an authenticated but unassigned user to `/create-account`.
- `src/app/global-admin/global-admin.routes.ts:5-49` currently exposes tenant and Email Outbox operations only.

**Fix direction**

Represent platform-administrator authority explicitly in request context, route guards, and server authorization rather than treating it as an ordinary tenant role. Allow the intended direct platform operations without a tenant membership, log each cross-tenant action with actor and target tenant, and keep user-context actions distinct from platform actions.

Use immutable audit entries with actor, target tenant, action, before/after data,
reason, and timestamp.

### CI-001 — CI excludes product-facing finance docs

**Severity:** P1
**Why it matters:** Finance/receipts are high-risk product workflows, and local documentation discovery includes them.

**Evidence**

- `.github/workflows/e2e-baseline.yml:182-187` runs the docs project with `--grep-invert "@finance"`.
- `tests/docs/finance/finance-overview.doc.ts:11`, `tests/docs/finance/receipt-review-reimbursement.doc.ts:14`, and `tests/docs/finance/inclusive-tax-rates.doc.ts` are therefore omitted, as is `tests/docs/profile/discounts.doc.ts:62`.
- `bun run test:e2e:docs -- --list` on this audit branch found those tests locally, proving the CI exclusion is not a discovery limitation.

**Fix direction**

Remove the inversion and repair any failure it reveals. If a real external dependency remains, split only that explicit integration path into a separate job and keep deterministic finance docs in baseline CI.

### TEST-001 — Manual approval has no complete page-backed journey

**Severity:** P1
**Why it matters:** The application/manual-approval mode now has participant application copy and organizer approval UI, but no durable flow proves the end-to-end behavior.

**Evidence**

- `src/app/events/event-registration-option/event-registration-option.component.ts:93-100` presents the application behavior.
- `src/app/events/event-organize/event-organize.html:79-126` exposes organizer approval.
- `tests/test-inventory.md:30-83` has no manual-approval spec/doc entry.

**Fix direction**

Add a seeded participant-application → organizer-approval → free confirmation or paid Checkout → outbox-visible path, with capacity and duplicate-approval negative cases. Generate product documentation from the same flow.

### TEST-002 — Existing-user role assignment is not verified in the browser
### TEST-002 — Existing-user role assignment is not verified in the browser

**Severity:** P1
**Why it matters:** Roles/capabilities are a core tenant safety boundary. The server handler is covered, but the discoverable UI has no durable mutation/readback proof.

**Evidence**

- The UI mutates assignments in `src/app/admin/user-list/user-list.component.ts:96-114`.
- `tests/specs/admin/roles-management.spec.ts:20-34` only asserts that role controls are visible.
- `tests/docs/roles/roles.doc.ts:42-75` likewise documents visibility rather than changing a role and reading it back.

**Fix direction**

Use a disposable role and tenant user in a functional spec and generated doc: change the selection, assert the persisted assignment and updated UI, clean up, and cover the unauthorized/read-only view.

## Stabilization and quality findings

### SEC-002 — Request `Origin` can select email and Checkout destinations

**Severity:** P2

`src/server/effect/rpc/app-rpcs.request-handler.ts:30-60` preserves original request headers. `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/events/event-registration.service.ts:86-96` trusts `headers['origin']` without validating it against the tenant, then uses it for approval emails (`:566`) and Stripe success/cancel URLs (`:801-817`). An authorized or compromised organizer can choose a phishing destination for an applicant.

**Fix direction:** persist a canonical root URL on the tenant record, validate
it against the active primary domain, and derive production public URLs from it.
Only a platform administrator may change the saved tenant host or root URL. Use
an explicit local runtime origin in development; never trust caller-controlled
`Origin`, forwarded-host, or request headers.

### OPS-001 — Claimed outbox messages can remain permanently stuck in `sending`

**Severity:** P2

`src/server/notifications/email-delivery.ts:307-326` changes a row to `sending`; later polling at `:328-344` selects only `queued` and `failed`. A crash after claim and before terminal update loses the notification indefinitely.

**Fix direction:** add a claim lease timestamp and stale-claim recovery. Reclaim safely with the existing Resend idempotency key and cover restart/crash recovery.

### DATA-001 — Important tenant boundaries lack database-level tuple constraints

**Severity:** P2

`src/db/schema/users.ts:96-109` can pair a role with a tenant membership from another tenant; handler checks reduce the risk, but request-context permission resolution can consume that role if bad data enters the database. Similarly, `src/db/schema/event-registrations.ts:16-40` independently stores tenant, event, and option references, while event options are keyed only to event in `src/db/schema/event-registration-options.ts:14-42`.

**Fix direction:** model tenant-aware composite keys/foreign keys or use database-enforced tuple checks. Preserve handler validation as defense in depth and add direct constraint tests.

### UX-001 — Template pages expose actions that direct server guards will deny

**Severity:** P2

- `src/app/templates/template-list/template-list.component.html:12-24` always shows “Manage categories”; `src/app/templates/categories/category-list/category-list.component.html:8-17,76-82` always exposes creation/edit actions.
- `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/template-categories.handlers.ts:65-67,101-103` correctly requires `templates:manageCategories`.

import {
  boolean,
  integer,
  pgEnum,
  pgTable,
  text,
  varchar,
} from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';

import { eventInstances } from './event-instances';
import { eventRegistrations } from './event-registrations';
import { modelOfTenant } from './model';
import { currencyEnum } from './tenants';
import { users } from './users';

export const transactionStatus = pgEnum('transaction_status', [
  'pending',
  'successful',
  'cancelled',
]);

export const transactionMethod = pgEnum('transaction_method', [
  'stripe',
  'transfer',
  'paypal',
  'cash',
]);

export const transactionType = pgEnum('transaction_type', [
  'registration',
  'refund',
  'other',
]);

export const transactions = pgTable('transactions', {
  ...modelOfTenant,
  amount: integer().notNull(),
  appFee: integer(),
  comment: text(),
  currency: currencyEnum().notNull(),
  eventId: varchar({ length: 20 }).references(() => eventInstances.id),
  eventRegistrationId: varchar({ length: 20 }).references(
    () => eventRegistrations.id,
  ),
  executiveUserId: varchar({ length: 20 }).references(() => users.id),
  manuallyCreated: boolean().default(false),
  method: transactionMethod().notNull(),
  status: transactionStatus().notNull(),
  stripeChargeId: varchar().unique(),
  stripeCheckoutSessionId: varchar().unique(),
  stripeCheckoutUrl: varchar().unique(),
  stripeFee: integer(),
  stripePaymentIntentId: varchar().unique(),
  targetUserId: varchar({ length: 20 }).references(() => users.id),
  type: transactionType().notNull(),
});
import {
  boolean,
  integer,
  pgTable,
  text,
  timestamp,
  varchar,
} from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';

import { eventInstances } from './event-instances';
import { eventRegistrationOptions } from './event-registration-options';
import { discountTypes, registrationStatus } from './global-enums';
import { modelOfTenant } from './model';
import { users } from './users';

export const eventRegistrations = pgTable('event_registrations', {
  appliedDiscountedPrice: integer('applied_discounted_price'),
  appliedDiscountType: discountTypes('applied_discount_type'),
  basePriceAtRegistration: integer('base_price_at_registration'),
  ...modelOfTenant,
  checkedInGuestCount: integer('checked_in_guest_count').notNull().default(0),
  checkInTime: timestamp(),
  discountAmount: integer('discount_amount'),
  eventId: varchar({ length: 20 })
    .notNull()
    .references(() => eventInstances.id),
  guestCount: integer('guest_count').notNull().default(0),
  paymentId: varchar({ length: 255 }),
  registrationOptionId: varchar({ length: 20 })
    .notNull()
    .references(() => eventRegistrationOptions.id),
  status: registrationStatus().notNull(),
  stripeTaxRateId: varchar('tax_rate_id'),
  taxRateDisplayName: text('tax_rate_name'),
  taxRateInclusive: boolean('tax_rate_inclusive'),
  taxRatePercentage: text('tax_rate_percentage'),
  userId: varchar({ length: 20 })
    .notNull()
    .references(() => users.id),
});

# Features

Public package and module surface area to be aware of when researching or implementing a solution. Each module entry includes its vendored repo path so it can be located quickly.

## `effect` Package

Package path: `packages/effect`

- `Array` - `packages/effect/src/Array.ts` - Immutable array utilities. Use: transform arrays immutably.
- `BigDecimal` - `packages/effect/src/BigDecimal.ts` - Arbitrary-precision decimal arithmetic. Use: avoid floating-point errors.
- `BigInt` - `packages/effect/src/BigInt.ts` - `bigint` utilities and instances. Use: work with large integers.
- `Boolean` - `packages/effect/src/Boolean.ts` - Boolean utilities and instances. Use: compose boolean logic.
- `Brand` - `packages/effect/src/Brand.ts` - Branded type helpers. Use: prevent accidental type mixing.
- `Cache` - `packages/effect/src/Cache.ts` - Effect cache utilities. Use: memoize effectful lookups.
- `Cause` - `packages/effect/src/Cause.ts` - Structured effect failure representation. Use: inspect failures and defects.
- `Channel` - `packages/effect/src/Channel.ts` - Bidirectional streaming I/O abstraction. Use: build stream pipelines.
- `ChannelSchema` - `packages/effect/src/ChannelSchema.ts` - Schema helpers for channels. Use: type channel protocols.
- `Chunk` - `packages/effect/src/Chunk.ts` - Immutable high-performance sequence. Use: efficient functional sequences.
- `Clock` - `packages/effect/src/Clock.ts` - Time service and sleep utilities. Use: schedule and measure time.
- `Combiner` - `packages/effect/src/Combiner.ts` - Combine two values of one type. Use: merge values consistently.
- `Config` - `packages/effect/src/Config.ts` - Schema-driven configuration loading. Use: read typed app config.

# Effect Guide

This guide is based on common usage patterns in the vendored repo at `./repos/effect`.

Key source areas:

- `./repos/effect/packages/effect/src/Effect.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/platform-*`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/vitest/`

## Mental Model

`Effect<A, E, R>` is the default way to represent application work.

It describes a computation that:

- succeeds with `A`
- fails with `E`
- requires services `R`

The repo consistently uses `Effect` as the main abstraction for:

- business workflows
- service methods
- platform integrations
- resource lifecycles
- tests

## Most Common Patterns In The Repo

The dominant usage pattern is:

1. use `Effect.gen` for workflows and orchestration
2. use `Effect.fn` for reusable effectful functions
3. use precise constructors such as `succeed`, `fail`, `sync`, `try`, and `tryPromise`
4. use `map`, `flatMap`, and `tap` for local transformations
5. access services in implementations and `provide*` only at edges
6. use `acquireRelease` and `scoped` for owned resources
7. use `catchTag` and `match` for typed recovery
8. use `run*` only at runtime boundaries

## Prefer `Effect.fn` For Reusable Operations

For reusable effectful operations, prefer `Effect.fn`.

```ts
import { Effect } from "effect";

const loadUser = Effect.fn("loadUser")(function* (userId: string) {
  return { id: userId, name: "Ada" };
});
```

Use `Effect.fn` when:

- the operation is reusable
- the operation takes parameters
- the operation is part of business logic or a module API
- you want consistent tracing and stack frames

Do not treat `Effect.fnUntraced` as the default. If you do not want an explicit named span, use `Effect.fn` without a span name.

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/utils/src/Codegen.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiPatch.ts`

## Use `Effect.gen` For Workflows

Use `Effect.gen` for orchestration and sequential workflows, especially when there are multiple `yield*` steps.

```ts
const program = Effect.gen(function* () {
  const config = yield* Config;
  const repo = yield* UserRepo;
  const user = yield* repo.getById("u_123");
  return { config, user };
});
```

Use `Effect.gen` when:

- the body is a workflow
- you are reading multiple services
- you have branching or multiple sequential steps
- you are implementing a layer, handler, or orchestration

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/NodeSdk.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiGenerator.ts`

## `Effect.fn` vs `Effect.gen`

Use this rule:

- reusable operation: `Effect.fn`
- inline workflow block: `Effect.gen`

Good split:

```ts
const loadUser = Effect.fn("loadUser")(function* (userId: string) {
  const repo = yield* UserRepo;
  return yield* repo.getById(userId);
});

const program = Effect.gen(function* () {
  const user = yield* loadUser("u_123");
  yield* Effect.logInfo("loaded user", user);
});
```

## `Effect.fnUntraced` Is An Escape Hatch

For application and business code, `Effect.fnUntraced` is not the default.

Use it only when:

- the function is an internal low-level helper
- observability is intentionally being traded away
- there is a concrete performance or tracing reason

If the only goal is to avoid an explicit named span, prefer:

```ts
const normalizeUser = Effect.fn(function* (input: string) {
  return input.trim().toLowerCase();
});
```

Instead of:

```ts
const normalizeUser = Effect.fnUntraced(function* (input: string) {
  return input.trim().toLowerCase();
});
```

## Constructor Functions

The repo uses constructor functions very deliberately.

### `Effect.succeed`

Use for pure successful values.

```ts
const ok = Effect.succeed(42);
```

### `Effect.fail`

Use for expected typed failures.

```ts
const notFound = Effect.fail(UserNotFound.make({ userId: "u_123" }));
```

### `Effect.sync`

Use for synchronous side effects or pure synchronous construction that should live inside `Effect`.

```ts
const buildConfig = Effect.sync(() => ({ retries: 3 }));
```

### `Effect.try`

Use for synchronous code that may throw.

```ts
import { Effect, Schema } from "effect";

class ParseError extends Schema.TaggedErrorClass<ParseError>()("ParseError", {
  cause: Schema.Defect,
}) {}

const parseJson = (input: string) =>
  Effect.try({
    try: () => JSON.parse(input),
    catch: (cause) => ParseError.make({ cause }),
  });
```

### `Effect.tryPromise`

Use for Promise-returning APIs.

```ts
import { Effect, Schema } from "effect";

class FetchError extends Schema.TaggedErrorClass<FetchError>()("FetchError", {
  cause: Schema.Defect,
}) {}

const fetchText = (url: string) =>
  Effect.tryPromise({
    try: () => fetch(url).then((response) => response.text()),
    catch: (cause) => FetchError.make({ cause }),
  });
```

Preferred rule:

- pure value: `succeed`
- expected failure: `fail`
- synchronous non-throwing effect: `sync`
- synchronous throwing boundary: `try`
- Promise boundary: `tryPromise`

## Local Composition

The repo uses `map`, `flatMap`, and `tap` constantly for small local transformations.

### `Effect.map`

Use to transform successful values.

```ts
const userName = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.map((user) => user.name));
```

### `Effect.flatMap`

Use when the next step returns another `Effect`.

```ts
const result = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.flatMap((user) => saveAudit(user.id)));
```

### `Effect.tap`

Use for side effects that should preserve the main value.

```ts
const result = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.tap((user) => Effect.logDebug("loaded user", { userId: user.id })));
```

Preferred rule:

- outer workflow: `Effect.gen`
- local transformation: `map`, `flatMap`, `tap`

## Services And Provisioning

Repo style is:

- access services in implementation code
- provide them at boundaries

### Access services in implementations

```ts
const loadUser = Effect.fn("loadUser")(function* (userId: string) {
  const repo = yield* UserRepo;
  return yield* repo.getById(userId);
});
```

or:

```ts
const loadUser = (userId: string) => Effect.service(UserRepo).pipe(Effect.flatMap((repo) => repo.getById(userId)));
```

### Provide at the edge

```ts
const program = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.provide(AppLayer));
```

Use `provideService` and `provideServiceEffect` for targeted overrides, especially in tests or framework boundaries.

Do not default to exporting thin accessor functions that just fetch a service and forward to one service method. Prefer real business operations or direct service usage within the owning workflow.

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/utils/src/bin.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/test/`

## Error Handling

Common repo patterns:

- `catchTag` for expected tagged errors
- `match` for totalizing an effect into a value
- `catchCause` for full-cause infra handling

### `Effect.catchTag`

Use for targeted typed recovery.

```ts
const safe = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.catchTag("UserNotFound", () => Effect.succeed(null)));
```

### `Effect.match`

Use when the caller wants a value either way.

```ts
const result = loadUser("u_123").pipe(
  Effect.match({
    onFailure: () => null,
    onSuccess: (user) => user,
  }),
);
```

For deeper guidance, see `./references/guide-error-handling.md`.

## Resource Management

One of the strongest repo patterns is explicit resource ownership.

### `Effect.acquireRelease`

Use for resources that must be cleaned up.

```ts
const connection = Effect.acquireRelease(openConnection, (conn) => closeConnection(conn));
```

### `Effect.scoped`

Use when a workflow consumes scoped resources and should tie cleanup to scope lifetime.

```ts
const program = Effect.scoped(
  Effect.gen(function* () {
    const conn = yield* connection;
    return yield* conn.query("select 1");
  }),
);
```

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/platform-node/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/NodeSdk.ts`

## SQL And Runtime Integrations

When Effect already provides a domain module for a capability, prefer that module over direct raw runtime client usage in business code.

Important example:

- prefer Effect SQL modules from `effect/unstable/sql/*` over embedding a native SQL driver directly in domain services

Why:

- transactions, spans, and typed errors stay inside the Effect model
- layering stays cleaner
- migrations and query conventions stay consistent

For SQL-specific guidance, see `./references/guide-sql.md`.

## Observability

The repo uses observability around meaningful boundaries, not every tiny helper.

Common patterns:

- `Effect.fn` for named operations
- `Effect.withSpan` for nested span boundaries
- `Effect.log*` for operational events
- `Effect.track` for metrics

For detailed guidance, see `./references/guide-observability.md`.

## Runtime Boundaries

The repo keeps `run*` APIs at true runtime boundaries.

### `Effect.runPromise`

Use when leaving Effect world into Promise-based hosts.

### `Effect.runFork`

Use for background fibers or long-running integration hooks.

### `Effect.runSync`

Use sparingly, mostly in specialized internals where synchrony is guaranteed.

Preferred rule:

- library/business code should return `Effect`
- entrypoints and integration boundaries should run `Effect`

If you have multiple external entrypoints, prefer `ManagedRuntime`.

## Commonly Used Effect APIs In This Repo

These are the most practically important `Effect` functions to know first:

- `Effect.fn`
- `Effect.gen`
- `Effect.succeed`
- `Effect.fail`
- `Effect.sync`
- `Effect.try`
- `Effect.tryPromise`
- `Effect.map`
- `Effect.flatMap`
- `Effect.tap`
- `Effect.service`
- `Effect.provide`
- `Effect.provideService`
- `Effect.catchTag`
- `Effect.match`
- `Effect.acquireRelease`
- `Effect.scoped`
- `Effect.withSpan`
- `Effect.logInfo`
- `Effect.logDebug`
- `Effect.runPromise`

## Good Repo Examples To Study

- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/utils/src/Codegen.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiPatch.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiGenerator.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/NodeSdk.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/Tracer.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/platform-node/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/vitest/src/index.ts`

Use for side effects that should preserve the main value.

```ts
const result = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.tap((user) => Effect.logDebug("loaded user", { userId: user.id })));
```

Preferred rule:

- outer workflow: `Effect.gen`
- local transformation: `map`, `flatMap`, `tap`

## Services And Provisioning

Repo style is:

- access services in implementation code
- provide them at boundaries

### Access services in implementations

```ts
const loadUser = Effect.fn("loadUser")(function* (userId: string) {
  const repo = yield* UserRepo;
  return yield* repo.getById(userId);
});
```

or:

```ts
const loadUser = (userId: string) => Effect.service(UserRepo).pipe(Effect.flatMap((repo) => repo.getById(userId)));
```

### Provide at the edge

```ts
const program = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.provide(AppLayer));
```

Use `provideService` and `provideServiceEffect` for targeted overrides, especially in tests or framework boundaries.

Do not default to exporting thin accessor functions that just fetch a service and forward to one service method. Prefer real business operations or direct service usage within the owning workflow.

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/utils/src/bin.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/test/`

## Error Handling

Common repo patterns:

- `catchTag` for expected tagged errors
- `match` for totalizing an effect into a value
- `catchCause` for full-cause infra handling

### `Effect.catchTag`

Use for targeted typed recovery.

```ts
const safe = loadUser("u_123").pipe(Effect.catchTag("UserNotFound", () => Effect.succeed(null)));
```

### `Effect.match`

Use when the caller wants a value either way.

```ts
const result = loadUser("u_123").pipe(
  Effect.match({
    onFailure: () => null,
    onSuccess: (user) => user,
  }),
);
```

For deeper guidance, see `./references/guide-error-handling.md`.

## Resource Management

One of the strongest repo patterns is explicit resource ownership.

### `Effect.acquireRelease`

Use for resources that must be cleaned up.

```ts
const connection = Effect.acquireRelease(openConnection, (conn) => closeConnection(conn));
```

### `Effect.scoped`

Use when a workflow consumes scoped resources and should tie cleanup to scope lifetime.

```ts
const program = Effect.scoped(
  Effect.gen(function* () {
    const conn = yield* connection;
    return yield* conn.query("select 1");
  }),
);
```

Repo examples:

- `./repos/effect/packages/platform-node/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/NodeSdk.ts`

## SQL And Runtime Integrations

When Effect already provides a domain module for a capability, prefer that module over direct raw runtime client usage in business code.

Important example:

- prefer Effect SQL modules from `effect/unstable/sql/*` over embedding a native SQL driver directly in domain services

Why:

- transactions, spans, and typed errors stay inside the Effect model
- layering stays cleaner
- migrations and query conventions stay consistent

For SQL-specific guidance, see `./references/guide-sql.md`.

## Observability

The repo uses observability around meaningful boundaries, not every tiny helper.

Common patterns:

- `Effect.fn` for named operations
- `Effect.withSpan` for nested span boundaries
- `Effect.log*` for operational events
- `Effect.track` for metrics

For detailed guidance, see `./references/guide-observability.md`.

## Runtime Boundaries

The repo keeps `run*` APIs at true runtime boundaries.

### `Effect.runPromise`

Use when leaving Effect world into Promise-based hosts.

### `Effect.runFork`

Use for background fibers or long-running integration hooks.

### `Effect.runSync`

Use sparingly, mostly in specialized internals where synchrony is guaranteed.

Preferred rule:

- library/business code should return `Effect`
- entrypoints and integration boundaries should run `Effect`

If you have multiple external entrypoints, prefer `ManagedRuntime`.

## Commonly Used Effect APIs In This Repo

These are the most practically important `Effect` functions to know first:

- `Effect.fn`
- `Effect.gen`
- `Effect.succeed`
- `Effect.fail`
- `Effect.sync`
- `Effect.try`
- `Effect.tryPromise`
- `Effect.map`
- `Effect.flatMap`
- `Effect.tap`
- `Effect.service`
- `Effect.provide`
- `Effect.provideService`
- `Effect.catchTag`
- `Effect.match`
- `Effect.acquireRelease`
- `Effect.scoped`
- `Effect.withSpan`
- `Effect.logInfo`
- `Effect.logDebug`
- `Effect.runPromise`

## Good Repo Examples To Study

- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/utils/src/Codegen.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiPatch.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/tools/openapi-generator/src/OpenApiGenerator.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/NodeSdk.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/opentelemetry/src/Tracer.ts`
- `./repos/effect/packages/platform-node/`
- `./repos/effect/packages/vitest/src/index.ts`

content.text:
Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/organizer_auth
Payload:
Finding `DSC-001-organizer-overview-authorization` remains valid on current HEAD `a6c8848`.

Evidence:

- The affected handler is unchanged since scan revision `9545a2c`.
- `events.getOrganizeOverview` only authenticates and tenant-scopes before querying attendee PII at `events-query.handlers.ts:829-1027`.
- A direct in-memory handler invocation as an authenticated ordinary member returned success and executed the protected query: `{"result":[],"sensitiveQueryCalled":true}`.
- Current targeted tests pass (`2 files, 11 tests`) but contain no authorization-denial coverage.

Patch contract:

- Preserve access for:
  - `events:organizeAll`
  - `finance:manageReceipts`
  - users with a same-tenant, same-event, `CONFIRMED` registration whose option has `organizingRegistration=true`
- Preserve `events.canOrganize` as a boolean query.
- Require a tenant user and evaluate that exact shared predicate before the overview registration query.
- A tenant member lacking the predicate must receive `RpcForbiddenError`; an authenticated session lacking a tenant user continues to receive `RpcUnauthorizedError`.
- Retain the existing tenant/event filter as defense in depth.
- Do not fetch or map attendee data before authorization succeeds.

Narrow implementation:

1. Extract the current `events.canOrganize` predicate into a private `Effect.fn` helper in `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/events/events-query.handlers.ts`.
2. Reuse it from both `events.canOrganize` and `events.getOrganizeOverview`.
3. Fail the overview with a clear `RpcForbiddenError`, such as `Event organizer access is required`, before `eventRegistrations.findMany`.
4. Add `EventsGetOrganizeOverviewRpcError = ForbiddenOrUnauthorizedRpcError` in `src/shared/rpc-contracts/app-rpcs/events.errors.ts` and use it for the overview RPC in `events.rpcs.ts`. The current `EventsRpcError` permits only unauthorized errors, so omitting this contract change would make the new forbidden failure invalid at the wire boundary.

Recommended tests:

- Add `src/server/effect/rpc/handlers/events/events-query.handlers.spec.ts`:
  - ordinary tenant member gets `RpcForbiddenError`, and protected `findMany` is not called;
  - authenticated session without tenant membership gets `RpcUnauthorizedError`, with no database call;
  - confirmed organizing registration succeeds;
  - `events:organizeAll` succeeds;
  - `finance:manageReceipts` succeeds;
  - `events.canOrganize` still returns false rather than failing for an ordinary member.
- Add a schema assertion in `events-rpcs.schema.spec.ts` that the overview error contract accepts `RpcForbiddenError`.
- Verify with targeted Vitest, full server unit tests, `build:app`, lint, formatting, and per-file diagnostics.

No user decision is required for the minimal security fix. The only policy watchpoint is that `finance:manageReceipts` currently grants organizer-page access; preserving it avoids changing existing product behavior. Splitting receipt access from attendee-roster access would be a separate least-privilege product decision.

No files were edited; the worktree remains clean.

/**
 * Converts a `URL` object's search parameters into a record.
 *
 * **Details**
 *
 * Repeated parameters are represented as arrays in insertion order.
 *
 * @category search params
 * @since 4.0.0
 */
export const searchParamsFromURL = (url: URL): ReadonlyRecord<string, string | Array<string>> => {
  const out: Record<string, string | Array<string>> = {}
  for (const [key, value] of url.searchParams.entries()) {
    const entry = out[key]
    if (entry !== undefined) {
      if (Array.isArray(entry)) {
        entry.push(value)
      } else {

    } else {
      out[key] = value

* import { Console, Effect, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const stream = Stream.make(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
 * const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
 *   yield* Stream.runForEachArray(
 *     stream,
 *     (chunk) => Console.log(`Processing chunk: ${chunk.join(", ")}`)
 *   )
 * })
 *
 * Effect.runPromise(program)
 * // Processing chunk: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 4.0.0
 */
export const runForEachArray: {
  <A, X, E2, R2>(
    f: (a: Arr.NonEmptyReadonlyArray<A>) => Effect.Effect<X, E2, R2>
  ): <E, R>(self: Stream<A, E, R>) => Effect.Effect<void, E2 | E, R2 | R>
  <A, E, R, X, E2, R2>(
    self: Stream<A, E, R>,
    f: (a: Arr.NonEmptyReadonlyArray<A>) => Effect.Effect<X, E2, R2>
  ): Effect.Effect<void, E | E2, R | R2>
} = dual(2, <A, E, R, X, E2, R2>(
  self: Stream<A, E, R>,
  f: (a: Arr.NonEmptyReadonlyArray<A>) => Effect.Effect<X, E2, R2>
): Effect.Effect<void, E | E2, R | R2> => Channel.runForEach(self.channel, f))

/**
 * Runs the stream for its effects, discarding emitted elements.
 *
 * **Example** (Draining a stream run)
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Console, Effect, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
 *   const stream = Stream.make(1, 2, 3).pipe(
 *     Stream.mapEffect((n) => Console.log(`Processing: ${n}`))
 *   )
 *
 *   yield* Stream.runDrain(stream)
 * })
 *
 * Effect.runPromise(program)
 * // Processing: 1
 * // Processing: 2
 * // Processing: 3
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 2.0.0
 */
export const runDrain = <A, E, R>(self: Stream<A, E, R>): Effect.Effect<void, E, R> => Channel.runDrain(self.channel)

/**
 * Returns a scoped pull for manually consuming the stream's output chunks.
 *
 * **Details**
 *
 * The pull fails with `Cause.Done` when the stream ends and with the stream
 * error on failure.
 *
 * **Example** (Creating a scoped pull)
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Console, Effect, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const stream = Stream.make(1, 2, 3)
 *
 * const program = Effect.scoped(
 *   Effect.gen(function*() {
 *     const pull = yield* Stream.toPull(stream)
 *     const chunk = yield* pull
 *     yield* Console.log(chunk)
 *   })
 * )
 *
 * Effect.runPromise(program)
 * // [1, 2, 3]
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 2.0.0
 */
export const toPull = <A, E, R>(
  self: Stream<A, E, R>
): Effect.Effect<Pull.Pull<Arr.NonEmptyReadonlyArray<A>, E>, never, R | Scope.Scope> => Channel.toPull(self.channel)

/**
 * Concatenates all emitted strings into a single string.
 *
 * **Example** (Joining strings from a stream)
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Console, Effect, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const stream = Stream.make("Hello", " ", "World", "!")
 * const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
 *   const text = yield* Stream.mkString(stream)
 *   yield* Console.log(text)
 * })
 *
 * Effect.runPromise(program)
 * // Hello World!
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 2.0.0
 */
export const mkString = <E, R>(self: Stream<string, E, R>): Effect.Effect<string, E, R> =>
  Channel.runFold(
    self.channel,
    () => "",
    (acc, chunk) => acc + chunk.join("")
  )

/**
 * Concatenates the stream's `Uint8Array` chunks into a single `Uint8Array`.

export const mkString = <E, R>(self: Stream<string, E, R>): Effect.Effect<string, E, R> =>
  Channel.runFold(
    self.channel,
    () => "",
    (acc, chunk) => acc + chunk.join("")
  )

/**
 * Concatenates the stream's `Uint8Array` chunks into a single `Uint8Array`.
 *
 * **Example** (Joining Uint8Array chunks)
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Console, Effect, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const stream = Stream.make(new Uint8Array([1, 2]), new Uint8Array([3, 4]))
 * const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
 *   const bytes = yield* Stream.mkUint8Array(stream)
 *   yield* Console.log([...bytes])
 * })
 *
 * Effect.runPromise(program)
 * // [1, 2, 3, 4]
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 4.0.0
 */
export const mkUint8Array = <E, R>(self: Stream<Uint8Array, E, R>): Effect.Effect<Uint8Array, E, R> =>
  Effect.map(
    Channel.runFold(
      self.channel,
      (): {
        bytes: number
        readonly arrays: Array<Uint8Array>
      } => ({
        bytes: 0,
        arrays: []
      }),
      (acc, chunk) => {
        for (let i = 0; i < chunk.length; i++) {
          acc.bytes += chunk[i].length
          acc.arrays.push(chunk[i])
        }
        return acc
      }
    ),
    ({ arrays, bytes }) => {
      const result = new Uint8Array(bytes)
      let offset = 0
      for (let i = 0; i < arrays.length; i++) {
        const array = arrays[i]
        result.set(array, offset)
        offset += array.length
      }
      return result
    }
  )

/**
 * Converts the stream to a `ReadableStream` using the provided services.
 *
 * **When to use**
 *
 * Use when bridging to Web Streams and you already have the `Context` required
 * to run the stream outside an `Effect`.
 *
 * **Details**
 *
 * See https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/ReadableStream.
 *
 * **Example** (Converting to a ReadableStream with services)
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Context, Stream } from "effect"
 *
 * const stream = Stream.make(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
 * const readableStream = Stream.toReadableStreamWith(stream, Context.empty())
 * ```
 *
 * @category destructors
 * @since 4.0.0
 */
export const toReadableStreamWith = dual<
  <A, XR>(
    context: Context.Context<XR>,
    options?: { readonly strategy?: QueuingStrategy<A> | undefined }
  ) => <E, R extends XR>(self: Stream<A, E, R>) => ReadableStream<A>,
  <A, E, XR, R extends XR>(
    self: Stream<A, E, R>,
    context: Context.Context<XR>,
    options?: { readonly strategy?: QueuingStrategy<A> | undefined }
  ) => ReadableStream<A>
>(

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/origin_urls
Payload:
DSC-008 remains reachable on current `HEAD`; the scan commit and current implementation are unchanged in the affected files.

### Remediation specification

- Add required `canonicalRootUrl` to the tenant Drizzle model, `Tenant` schema, global-admin tenant records, and create/update RPC inputs.
- Platform-admin create/edit must persist `domain` and `canonicalRootUrl` atomically. Normalize the URL to its origin and require:
  - absolute `https:` URL;
  - hostname exactly equals the normalized primary `domain`;
  - no credentials, non-default port, path beyond `/`, query, fragment, or trailing-dot ambiguity.
- Add the field to global-admin forms/detail views. Make it required, show it operationally, and use it for “Open tenant” instead of synthesizing `https://${domain}`.
- Seed canonical values as `https://${domain}`. For the local tenant, the stored value can be `https://localhost`; the explicit local runtime override supplies the actual HTTP port.

Centralize outbound URL resolution:

- Use `BASE_URL` only when it parses as a root `http`/`https` loopback origin (`localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, or `::1`); allow its local port.
- If `BASE_URL` is absent or is a valid non-loopback production value, use the tenant’s validated `canonicalRootUrl`.
- A malformed `BASE_URL`, malformed canonical URL, or canonical/domain mismatch fails closed with `EventRegistrationInternalError`; never fall back to `Origin`, `Host`, forwarded headers, or an empty string.
- Build event and status URLs with `new URL`/`searchParams`, encoding the event ID rather than concatenating strings.

Remove the untrusted source completely:

- Delete `resolveRequestOrigin`.
- Remove `headers` from `ApproveManualRegistrationArguments` and `RegisterForEventArguments`.
- Stop passing `options.headers` from `events.approveRegistration` and `events.registerForEvent`.
- Pass `tenant.domain` and `tenant.canonicalRootUrl` into the service.
- Resolve the trusted URL before any registration/capacity/outbox mutation:
  - every manual approval needs it for the email;
  - only paid immediate registration needs it during self-registration.
- Preserve the public event RPC payloads. The only public contract change is the required global-admin tenant field.

### Tests

- URL policy unit tests: canonical normalization, exact-host enforcement, HTTPS requirement, path/query/credential/port rejection, loopback `BASE_URL` override, non-loopback `BASE_URL` ignored, and invalid configuration failure.
- Global-admin tests: canonical URL is required, normalized, returned, and rejected before DB mutation when mismatched with the domain.
- Security regressions through both duplicate sinks:
  - self-paid registration sends canonical success/cancel URLs to Stripe despite forged `Origin`, `Host`, and forwarded headers;
  - paid manual approval uses canonical Stripe and email URLs;
  - free manual approval email uses the canonical URL;
  - invalid tenant URL configuration causes no Stripe call, outbox insert, capacity change, or registration mutation.
- Update tenant/context/form/list fixtures and run focused Vitest, full unit tests, lint, formatting, and app build.

### Dependencies and rollout

- DSC-006 must also prevent tenant roles from acquiring `globalAdmin:manageTenants`; otherwise the intended platform-only URL/domain administration boundary is not trustworthy.
- No product decision is still open: the latest product, architecture, and compliance docs explicitly establish this policy.
- Follow the prior relaunch decision: change the new Drizzle schema directly and do not add `migration/steps/**` work. Existing target-schema data must be reset or populated by the separate data-transfer process before deployment; never add a request-derived compatibility fallback.

No files were changed and no tests were run during this Plan-mode investigation.

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/self_assignment
Payload:
DSC-016 remains present on current HEAD `a6c8848`; the scan commit is its ancestor and none of the relevant files changed.

Validation rubric:

- [x] `users.assignRoles` is remotely callable by a limited capability holder.
- [x] A non-empty self-assignment passes current checks.
- [x] `roles.findMany` exposes every tenant role ID to that holder.
- [x] The handler replaces role links, and refreshed request context flattens the acquired permissions.
- [x] The role form can create a limited role containing `users:assignRoles` while a stronger Admin role coexists.

This is a complete static attack path. The only unresolved fact is product policy, not reachability.

## Required decision

Ask:

> Should `users:assignRoles` support bounded delegation to limited role managers, or should possession of it be treated as full tenant-administrator authority?

- **Bounded delegation (recommended):** prohibit every self-role change and reject any requested final role set whose effective tenant capabilities exceed the actor’s.
- **Full admin authority:** arbitrary assignment, including acquisition of Admin through another account, is intentional; document the capability as equivalent to full tenant control.

“Block self-assignment only” is not sufficient: the holder can grant Admin to a second account they control.

Evidence is mixed enough that this should be confirmed:

- The original decision requires role assignment while preserving role/capability boundaries.
- `users:assignRoles` is deliberately separate from `admin:manageRoles`, suggesting delegation.
- The metadata says it assigns existing roles “without changing role definitions.”
- Conversely, the default seed grants it only to Admin, and no hierarchy/ceiling is documented.

## Decision-complete implementation for the recommended option

- Add a shared pure helper that determines whether the final requested roles’ **effective tenant permissions** are a subset of the actor’s effective permissions. Evaluate the canonical tenant permission universe with `includesPermission`; do not compare raw arrays because dependencies, legacy aliases, and wildcards affect authority.
- Reuse the tenant-only permission schema introduced for DSC-006. Any role containing a platform-global permission must fail independently of the ceiling.
- In `users.assignRoles`:
  - keep the existing permission, membership, and tenant-role checks;
  - reject every `userId === currentUser.id` request before any write;
  - load requested role IDs plus permissions inside the transaction;
  - union the final requested permissions and enforce the ceiling;
  - only then delete/insert assignments.
- Preserve current removal semantics: a limited manager may demote another user to a final role set within their ceiling. Do not add a separate “cannot edit stronger target” hierarchy without another product decision.
- Replace `UserSelfRoleRemovalError` with a typed `UserRoleAssignmentForbiddenError` carrying `reason: "self" | "capabilityCeiling"` and clear messages.
- Add `isCurrentUser` to `UsersFindManyRecord`; render the actor’s row read-only with “Your roles must be changed by another role manager.”
- Explain the ceiling on the page and in generated role documentation. On any rejected mutation, refetch the user list so the multi-select cannot display an unpersisted role set.
- No database schema or migration change is needed.

## Tests

- Self assignment with empty and non-empty sets fails before delete/insert.
- A limited assigner cannot grant Admin to another account.
- Equal/weaker, dependency-equivalent, and legacy-alias-equivalent grants succeed.
- A multi-role final set fails atomically if any effective capability exceeds the actor.
- Cross-tenant/missing-role behavior remains unchanged.
- UI shows the actor’s row read-only with an explanation and restores persisted state after rejection.
- Add a focused Playwright scenario using a limited assigner plus stronger Admin role, and update generated role docs.
- Update the currently stale shared permission spec that still expects `users:assignRoles` to be labeled “future.”

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/stripe_webhook
Payload:
Both findings remain confirmed on HEAD `a6c8848`; the relevant code and tests are byte-identical to scan revision `9545a2c`.

### DSC-004 — Checkout completion binding

Current path:

- `resolveCheckoutSession` treats complete metadata as authoritative and never requires `eventSession.id === transactions.stripeCheckoutSessionId`.
- The completed handler tolerates a PaymentIntent missing from the target tenant account.
- The pending transaction update is not checked before confirming the registration.
- `event.account` is ignored, although Stripe states every Connect event identifies its originating connected account at the top level. [Stripe Connect webhooks](https://docs.stripe.com/connect/webhooks)
- The same metadata trust exists in `checkout.session.expired`, enabling an adjacent unauthorized-cancellation variant.

Recommended narrow fix:

- Resolve both completed and expired sessions from the unique persisted `stripeCheckoutSessionId`; metadata becomes corroborating data only.
- Accept metadata only when all three IDs are absent, or all three exactly match the persisted transaction, tenant, and registration. Reject partial/conflicting metadata.
- Require `event.account` to be present and equal the current tenant `stripeAccountId`; fail closed on absent/mismatched account.
- For completion, require a PaymentIntent ID, reject a mismatch with a stored ID or another transaction, and retain connected-account retrieval for string IDs. A `resource_missing` response must become HTTP 400 rather than a tolerated `null`. An expanded PaymentIntent can remain usable after the strict event-account/session checks.
- Update the pending Stripe registration transaction with predicates for transaction ID, tenant, registration, method/type, persisted session ID, status, and compatible PaymentIntent; use `returning` and confirm the registration only when exactly one transaction row changed. Return a retryable non-2xx conflict if no row changed.
- Apply the same session/account binding and update-before-cancel rule to `checkout.session.expired`.
- No schema or migration is required; existing unique session and PaymentIntent columns are sufficient.

HTTP behavior can remain text-based: 400 for missing/conflicting session, account, metadata, or PaymentIntent; 409 for a transaction-state race; 200 for valid processing and existing dedupe outcomes.

### DSC-010 — Webhook body buffering

Current path:

- `/webhooks/stripe` converts the Effect request to the original Bun `Request` without buffering.
- The handler then calls `request.arrayBuffer()` before enforcing 200 KiB.
- Bun’s server-wide `maxRequestBodySize` remains at its 128 MiB default. [Bun Serve API](https://bun.sh/reference/bun/Serve)

Recommended narrow fix:

- Keep the public handler signature unchanged, but replace `arrayBuffer()` with a route-local bounded reader.
- Reject a valid numeric `Content-Length` above 200 KiB before consuming the body.
- For missing/invalid length and chunked requests, read `request.body` incrementally, track cumulative bytes, cancel/release the reader immediately after crossing the cap, and allocate the final contiguous buffer only after a body at or below the cap finishes.
- Model size overflow as an expected result mapped to 413; wrap unexpected stream failures in a typed internal body-read error for existing server logging.
- Do not set Bun’s global limit to 200 KiB: RPC currently supports 5 MiB brand assets and 20 MiB receipt originals, with base64 overhead.

### Tests

Extend the existing Stripe replay suite to cover:

- valid completion with matching persisted session, event account, metadata, and PaymentIntent;
- fully absent metadata resolving through the persisted session;
- foreign session metadata, mismatched/missing event account, conflicting PaymentIntent, and missing target-account PaymentIntent all leaving transaction, registration, and capacity unchanged;
- zero-row transaction update never confirming registration;
- expired events requiring the same persisted session/account binding;
- existing event-ID replay and stale-claim behavior.

Add focused bounded-reader unit tests for early `Content-Length` rejection, chunked overflow cancellation, exact-limit byte preservation, and empty bodies. Add one route-level oversized request asserting 413 before signature processing.

No user decision is required. The recommended default is fail-closed use of the tenant’s current Stripe account; changing a tenant account while checkouts are pending may strand those sessions, which is already consistent with current refund/expiry behavior. Persisting historical account IDs would be a separate schema feature.

No files were edited and no tests were run.

payload.message: The repository resolves eight findings without product input. Two policy choices remain: whether role assignment is a bounded delegated capability or full tenant-admin authority, and whether arbitrary Icons8 additions remain part of authoring. I’ve narrowed both to concrete options; these choices change the security boundary and user experience, so I need to lock them before the final implementation plan.

Two policy choices remain: whether role assignment is a bounded delegated capability or full tenant-admin authority, and whether arbitrary Icons8 additions remain part of authoring. I’ve narrowed both to concrete options; these choices change the security boundary and user experience, so I need to lock them before the final implementation plan.

payload.arguments: {"questions":[{"header":"Role policy","id":"role_assignment_policy","question":"What authority should `users:assignRoles` grant?","options":[{"label":"Bounded delegation (Recommended)","description":"Block all self changes and allow assigning only roles whose effective tenant capabilities do not exceed the actor’s."},{"label":"Full tenant admin","description":"Treat the capability as full tenant control, allow arbitrary role assignment, and document that trust boundary explicitly."}]},{"header":"Icon source","id":"icon_authoring_policy","question":"Should content authors keep the ability to add arbitrary Icons8 icons directly?","options":[{"label":"Keep, resource-bound (Recommended)","description":"Retain direct-add but require a valid event/template/category authoring context and enforce server-side ownership or capability checks."},{"label":"Seeded catalog only","description":"Remove direct-add and restrict authors to icons already present in the tenant catalog."},{"label":"New icon permission","description":"Add a dedicated tenant capability that separately controls who may extend the icon catalog."}]},{"header":"Icon limit","id":"icon_catalog_limit_policy","question":"When a bounded tenant icon catalog reaches its limit, how should additions behave?","options":[{"label":"Evict old unused (Recommended)","description":"Automatically remove the oldest unreferenced catalog entries while preserving icons currently used by events, templates, or categories."},{"label":"Reject at quota","description":"Block additions at the limit and require an explicit tenant-admin cleanup workflow before more icons can be added."}]}]}

payload.output: {"answers":{"icon_authoring_policy":{"answers":["Whoever can access the icon dialog, can also add new icons, only when they are valid icons though. For now, we will not put a limit, it is unlikely that users really use that many icons"]},"icon_catalog_limit_policy":{"answers":["No need here, we allow unlimited icons"]},"role_assignment_policy":{"answers":["Full tenant admin"]}}}

content.text:
Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/global_permissions
Payload:
DSC-006 remains validated on current `main`; the affected code is unchanged since the scan.

## Patch contract

- Source: a tenant user with `admin:manageRoles` submits `globalAdmin:manageTenants` or `globalAdmin:*` through `admin.roles.create` or `admin.roles.update`.
- Persistence: both handlers store `input.permissions` unchanged.
- Materialization: each subsequent HTTP request reloads assigned role permissions and merges them with Auth0-derived platform permissions.
- Sink: `includesPermission` honors `globalAdmin:*`, and global-admin handlers trust the merged `globalAdmin:manageTenants`.
- No login/session renewal is required—only a new request.
- Updating an already-assigned role is the shortest path. Creating a poisoned `defaultUserRole` gives a second path through normal tenant onboarding without `users:assignRoles`.

The invariant is unambiguous from `PRODUCT.md` and `ARCHITECTURE.md`: platform administrators are platform principals, not tenant roles. Platform-global authority may originate only from Auth0 app metadata or the development/test-only configured override.

I reproduced both broken boundaries:

- `AdminRolesCreateInput` accepted a default role containing `globalAdmin:manageTenants`.
- `resolveRequestPermissions` accepted the same literal from tenant-user permissions and returned it as effective authority.

## Recommended implementation

- Split the shared permission contract:

  - Add a `TenantRolePermissionSchema` and inferred `TenantRolePermission` type containing exactly the tenant-role literals currently accepted (`ALL_PERMISSIONS` plus the legacy `admin:manageTaxes` alias).
  - Keep the broader `PermissionSchema` for effective request/context permissions because legitimate platform administrators still require global literals.
  - Do not broaden tenant-role writes to currently unsupported tenant wildcard literals as part of this security patch.

- Use the tenant-role schema end to end for:

  - `admin.roles.create` permissions.
  - A named/exported `AdminRolesUpdateInput` permissions schema.
  - Admin role records.
  - The Drizzle role-column TypeScript type.
  - Role-form data types where compilation requires it.

- Separate permission origins during request-context resolution:

  - Validate/partition each stored role’s JSON before normalization.
  - Exclude only invalid/platform-global entries from tenant-role materialization, preserving legitimate tenant permissions and other roles.
  - Emit a structured security warning with tenant ID, role ID, user ID, and rejected values.
  - Continue deriving global permissions solely in `resolveGlobalAdminPermissions`.

- Normalize admin role read results through the same helper so poisoned rows cannot violate the narrowed response schema. Create/update remain unchanged for legitimate values and invalid RPC payloads fail at schema decoding before any database call.

This is narrower than rejecting an entire contaminated role and better preserves legitimate tenant access while completely removing platform authority.

## Existing data and persistence

Run an operational audit for both global literals:

```sql
SELECT r.id, r."tenantId", r.name, p.permission
FROM roles AS r
CROSS JOIN LATERAL jsonb_array_elements_text(r.permissions)
  AS p(permission)
WHERE p.permission IN ('globalAdmin:*', 'globalAdmin:manageTenants');
```

If rows exist, remove only those entries transactionally and verify the query returns zero rows:

```sql
UPDATE roles
SET permissions =
  permissions - 'globalAdmin:*' - 'globalAdmin:manageTenants'
WHERE permissions ?| ARRAY[
  'globalAdmin:*',
  'globalAdmin:manageTenants'
]
RETURNING id, "tenantId", name, permissions;
```

Do not add an immediate validated Postgres check constraint: Fly applies Drizzle schema before the application deploy, so an existing poisoned row could block deployment. The runtime origin filter closes the exploit independently; a database constraint can follow only after every deployed environment audits clean. No legacy-to-new migration code is needed.

## Verification contract

Add focused regression coverage:

- `TenantRolePermissionSchema` accepts supported tenant capabilities and the legacy tax alias, but rejects both global literals.
- Actual create and update RPC payload schemas reject both global variants, including `defaultUserRole: true`.
- The broader effective-permission schema still accepts legitimate global-admin values.
- A mocked persisted role containing mixed tenant/global permissions retains tenant permissions but contributes no global authority.
- A clean Auth0 platform administrator still receives `globalAdmin:manageTenants`, with or without tenant membership.
- Legitimate role create/update persistence remains tenant-scoped.
- Existing role-management Playwright coverage still passes.

Run:

- Focused server and contract unit tests.
- `bun run test:unit`.
- `bun run lint`.
- `bun run format:write`.
- `bun run build:app`.
- `bun run test:e2e -- tests/specs/admin/roles-management.spec.ts`.
- Repeat the two pre-fix reproductions and confirm contract decoding now fails and tenant-sourced global authority is absent.
- Record commands, results, cleanup status, and remaining uncertainty in the scan’s `artifacts/fix_report.md`.

## Genuine user input

No product-policy decision remains. The only external authorization needed is whether execution may inspect and, if necessary, clean the deployed production database. The code fix should not wait for that access because its runtime origin filter safely neutralizes existing poisoned rows.

Current targeted baseline: `18` tests passed across the applicable server suites; the worktree remains unchanged.

Message Type: MESSAGE
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/icon_abuse
Payload:

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/receipt_keys
Payload:
DSC-002 remains valid on current HEAD (`a6c8848`); the relevant implementation is unchanged from the scanned commit.

Validation evidence:

- `FinanceReceiptAttachmentInput` accepts arbitrary `storageKey` and `storageUrl`.
- `finance.receipts.submit` persists both after only event permission and MIME checks.
- `byEvent`, `findOneForApproval`, and `refundableGroupedByRecipient` pass the stored key to the generic R2 GET presigner.
- The normal tenant/event/user key prefix is only a client-convention path, not a submit or signing control.
- Targeted finance tests pass: 2 files, 18 tests. They contain no arbitrary-key rejection case.
- Confidence remains high; exploitation requires organizer/finance submission access plus knowledge of another object key.

Recommended smallest complete design:

1. Add `finance_receipt_uploads` with:

   - `id`, `tenantId`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`
   - `eventId`, `uploadedByUserId`
   - `originalFileName`, `mimeType`, `sizeBytes`
   - unique `storageKey`, `storageUrl`
   - nullable `uploadedAt`, nullable `consumedAt`

   Add `financeReceipts.attachmentUploadId` as a non-null, unique FK. Keep the existing receipt attachment metadata/key columns as server-owned snapshots for the smallest read-model change.

2. Change the RPC contract:

   - Keep `finance.receiptMedia.uploadOriginal` input unchanged.
   - Return only `{ uploadId }`; do not return a reusable storage capability.
   - Change receipt submission attachment input to `{ fileName, uploadId }`.
   - Remove client-supplied MIME, size, storage key, and storage URL from submit input.
   - Existing receipt response shapes can remain unchanged for compatibility.

3. Make upload a persisted preflight:

   - Authorize tenant, event, user, and receipt-submission capability.
   - Validate MIME, decoded size, and base64 before persistence.
   - Generate the upload ID and key server-side, ideally `receipts/<tenant>/<event>/<user>/<uploadId>/<sanitized-name>`.
   - Insert the pending upload association before writing object storage.
   - Write the object, then mark the exact row `uploadedAt` and save the actual key/URL, including the local-unavailable sentinel.
   - Return `uploadId` only after that update succeeds. Failed writes leave an unconsumable row because `uploadedAt` remains null.

4. Consume atomically during submission:

   - Recheck current event permission and event existence.
   - In one DB transaction, conditionally set `consumedAt` only where upload ID, tenant, event, uploader, `uploadedAt IS NOT NULL`, and `consumedAt IS NULL` all match.
   - Use returned server-owned metadata/key/URL for the receipt insert and link `attachmentUploadId`.
   - If no row is returned, fail with one non-enumerating `RpcBadRequestError` such as `invalid_receipt_upload`.
   - The unique receipt upload FK is defense in depth against replay; transaction rollback restores consumption if receipt insertion fails.
   - Add an explicit UI `onError` notification instructing the organizer to upload the attachment again.

5. Fail closed before preview signing:

   - For all three signing reads, join the upload through `attachmentUploadId` and require exact tenant, event, submitted-user, and storage-key equality plus uploaded/consumed state.
   - Sign the key selected from that bound upload row, never an unverified receipt field.
   - Also verify the server-generated scope prefix before calling R2.
   - A missing/mismatched association returns `previewImageUrl: null` and emits a structured warning; the receipt remains visible.

Cleanup and compatibility:

- No expiry or object-deletion job is required to close this vulnerability. Failed and abandoned uploads remain a storage-cost follow-up; the current R2 integration has no delete primitive.
- The accepted relaunch architecture already requires this persisted preflight/consume model, so no product decision remains about actor scope or one-time use.
- The repository’s established relaunch rule is direct new-schema development without migration files.
- If existing receipt data must survive, this becomes the only user decision: use an expand/transfer path. A separate TypeScript transfer must synthesize upload records only for legacy keys matching the exact tenant/event/user prefix; mismatches should be quarantined with preview disabled. Existing R2 objects need not move.
- Because Fly applies schema before deploying the app, preserving an active existing database would require nullable/additive rollout first; a clean relaunch/reset can use the final non-null schema directly.
- Coordinate the body-buffering fix: a 20 MiB decoded receipt becomes about 26.7 MiB base64 before JSON overhead, so this RPC needs a route-specific limit or the decoded cap must intentionally change.

Required regression coverage:

- Upload record is scoped and persisted before object write; failed upload is not consumable.
- Unknown, cross-tenant, wrong-event, wrong-user, pending, and already-consumed upload IDs all fail identically.
- Submitted receipt metadata/key come from the upload row, not the request.
- Concurrent reuse permits exactly one receipt.
- Bound preview signs; mismatched/unbound association never calls the presigner.
- Existing organizer receipt submission Playwright flow remains green.
- Seed/docs fixtures create linked local-unavailable upload records.

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/icon_abuse
Payload:
DSC-019 remains valid on current HEAD; relevant code is unchanged since the scan commit.

Recommended defaults:

- Resource-bound authoring authority using existing event/template/category permissions and ownership.
- Icon names normalized to lowercase, max 128 characters, max 96 per segment, ASCII alphanumeric/hyphen with one optional colon.
- Search max 64 characters and 50 results.
- PostgreSQL-backed limits: 10 attempts per actor/tenant per 10 minutes and 50 per tenant/hour.
- Two concurrent process-wide resolutions, failing fast when saturated.
- Five-second fetch timeout, 256 KiB response cap, exact PNG/128×128 validation, and no insertion on resolution failure.
- Effect LRU cache: 256 entries, six-hour success TTL, 30-second failure TTL.
- Tenant catalog: 256 rows, current 25 defaults pinned, oldest custom row evicted transactionally.
- Typed forbidden, unavailable, rate-limited, and catalog-full errors shown inline.

The two product decisions to ask are:

1. Who may add Icons8 icons?

   - Current author, bound to the relevant operation — recommended.
   - A new `icons:manage` permission.
   - Nobody; seeded icons only.

2. At 256 catalog entries:

   - Evict the oldest custom icon — recommended; existing content remains intact because icons are stored as JSON snapshots.
   - Block additions and add an admin-management/delete surface.

I sent the parent agent the complete API shape, database/concurrency sequence, evidence, and test matrix.

Message Type: MESSAGE
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/registration_races
Payload:

Message Type: FINAL_ANSWER
Task name: /root
Sender: /root/registration_races
Payload:
Outcome: both findings remain valid on HEAD `a6c8848`; no files were changed.

### Required invariants

1. Active registration exclusivity:

```text
For each (tenant_id, event_id, user_id),
at most one event_registrations row may have status != CANCELLED.
```

This includes `PENDING`, `CONFIRMED`, and `WAITLIST`, preserving current mutual exclusivity.

2. Live checkout ownership:

```text
For each (tenant_id, event_registration_id),
at most one transactions row may have
type = registration AND status = pending.
```

A pending payment transaction owns the capacity/add-on reservation and Stripe initialization.

### Schema changes

- Add a concurrent partial unique index to `event_registrations`:

```sql
UNIQUE (tenant_id, event_id, user_id)
WHERE status <> 'CANCELLED'
```

Suggested name: `event_registrations_active_user_event_unique_idx`.

- Add a concurrent partial unique index to `transactions`:

```sql
UNIQUE (tenant_id, event_registration_id)
WHERE event_registration_id IS NOT NULL
  AND type = 'registration'
  AND status = 'pending'
```

Suggested name: `transactions_pending_registration_unique_idx`.

Use Drizzle `uniqueIndex(...).where(...).concurrently()`. No RPC/schema-contract changes are needed.

### DSC-018 patch flow

Current reads at `event-registration.service.ts:921-941`, `1154-1172`, and `1799-1815` are nonlocking; the schema has no uniqueness guard.

- In both `registerForEvent` and `joinWaitlist`, acquire `FOR UPDATE` on the caller’s `users_to_tenants` membership row before the transactional active-registration read. This serializes the same tenant/user across event registrations and also makes the existing tenant-wide active-registration-limit check reliable across concurrent different-event requests.
- Keep the partial unique index as the durable last line of defense.
- Insert registrations with `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ... RETURNING id`. A zero-row result must fail the transaction with the existing `EventRegistrationConflictError("User is already registered for this event")`; it must not commit a capacity or waitlist-counter change.
- Apply the same target-membership lock and in-transaction active-row recheck to transfers. Return the existing `"Target user already has an active registration"` conflict when the target won; retain not-found semantics only when the source row itself disappeared or changed.
- Preserve current product policy: do not newly make waitlists count toward `maxActiveRegistrationsPerUser`; that inconsistency is outside this finding.

### DSC-020 claim flow

Current code checks pending transactions before the mutation (`347-477`), then reserves capacity while leaving the registration `PENDING` (`571-669`), and only inserts the transaction after Stripe creation (`798-867`).

For paid manual approval:

1. Validate Stripe configuration before claiming.
2. Generate the transaction ID.
3. In one database transaction:
   - Insert a pending registration transaction without Stripe session fields using `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ... RETURNING id`.
   - If no row is returned, fail with `EventRegistrationConflictError("Registration is already awaiting payment")`.
   - Only after winning the claim, reserve capacity and add-ons and persist the registration’s price/discount/tax snapshot.
   - Any capacity, add-on, or registration CAS failure must be a typed failure so the claim and reservations roll back together.
4. Commit the claim before calling Stripe.
5. Create Checkout with the existing idempotency key derived from the winning registration/transaction IDs.
6. Persist `stripeCheckoutSessionId`, URL, and PaymentIntent only by conditionally updating that same pending claim; require exactly one updated row. Enqueue the approval email in the same transaction.
7. On a persistence error after Stripe returned:
   - Re-read the claim; if the same session is already bound, treat the database commit as successful and idempotently ensure the email.
   - Otherwise expire the returned Stripe session first. Release capacity/add-ons and cancel the claim only after expiry is confirmed.
   - If Stripe creation or expiry has an ambiguous outcome, leave the claim and reservation in place, log tenant/registration/transaction identifiers, and return an explicit internal error. Failing closed prevents a second payable session.

Free manual approval can retain its current conditional `PENDING → CONFIRMED` transaction because the failed second update already rolls back capacity.

### Required coupling to DSC-004

DSC-020 is not fully closed while the webhook accepts metadata-only mappings. A session created but not stored could otherwise settle the pending claim.

The DSC-004 fix must land first or in the same patch:

- Resolve completion and expiry from persisted `stripeCheckoutSessionId`, not metadata.
- Require tenant, registration, transaction, connected Stripe account, method/type/status, and PaymentIntent consistency.
- Update exactly one pending transaction before confirming its linked registration, atomically.
- A webhook arriving before session binding must make no state change and return non-success so a later provider retry can succeed.
- Metadata remains correlation data, never authority.

### Tests

- Add a real two-connection PostgreSQL barrier test for the active-registration index: both transactions observe no row; competing inserts yield exactly one active row.
- Add the equivalent two-connection test for pending checkout claims: exactly one pending registration transaction survives.
- Add service-level tests proving:
  - registration and waitlist losers receive the existing conflict and do not increment counters;
  - concurrent transfer versus registration maps to the target-active conflict;
  - two approvals produce one claim, one capacity/add-on reservation, one Stripe creation, and one email;
  - the Stripe idempotency key uses the winning claim ID;
  - session-binding failure expires the session before releasing the claim;
  - ambiguous Stripe outcomes remain fail-closed.
- Coordinate webhook regression cases with DSC-004, including an unpersisted/orphan session.
- Run the server unit suite, disposable-Postgres concurrency tests, schema push, lint/format, and application build.

### Deployment compatibility and input

Both indexes are additive and compatible with the currently deployed app; the old app may return an error rather than create corruption during the short schema-first deployment window.

Before deployment, run duplicate preflights for both predicates. If either returns rows, schema push will fail and automatic cleanup is unsafe because payment state, capacity, and add-on reservations determine which record is canonical.

Recommended policy: block deployment and manually reconcile duplicates against Stripe, then rerun the queries. Do not automatically cancel “all but newest/oldest.” User input is needed only if duplicate rows actually exist and a canonical registration/payment cannot be determined from Stripe and audit state.

The integrity-first handling of ambiguous Stripe outcomes follows the existing Stripe-source-of-truth architecture; automatic recovery would require a larger durable checkout-command/outbox design and should not be silently substituted.

   212	- The implemented surface here included tenant-scoped existing-user role assignment behind `users:assignRoles`, scanner-nav visibility gating, manual approval as a supported registration mode, tenant ops settings for email sender/Stripe/account limits, and a dedicated email delivery boundary under `src/server/notifications/email-delivery.ts`. [Task 1]
   213	- Manual approval applications were implemented to stay pending without consuming capacity; approval then reserves/confirms capacity, and paid approvals create a 24-hour checkout window. [Task 1]
   214	- When broad behavior changes land, update the source-guard tests and docs alongside the implementation so old scope assertions do not keep failing. [Task 1]
   215	- Validation that passed here was `bun run format:write`, `bunx vitest run --config vitest.config.ts`, `bunx --bun node@24.15.0 ./node_modules/@angular/cli/bin/ng.js lint --fix`, and `NG_BUILD_PARTIAL_SSR=1 bunx --bun node@24.15.0 ./node_modules/@angular/cli/bin/ng.js build`. [Task 1]
   216	- `git town hack <branch>` works cleanly from detached `origin/main` in this repo and preserves the current worktree changes before commit/push. [Task 2]
   217	- `gh auth status` succeeded and `gh repo view --json nameWithOwner,defaultBranchRef` confirmed `evorto-app/app` with base `main`; the validated fallback path for PR creation was `gh pr create --draft --base main --head codex/relaunch-registration-decisions ...`. [Task 2]
   218	- Final publish metadata here was branch `codex/relaunch-registration-decisions`, commit `a654a9e2e08 feat: implement relaunch registration decisions`, draft PR `#83`, and URL `https://github.com/evorto-app/app/pull/83`. [Task 2]
